A **weapon**, **arm**, or **armament** is any implement or device that is used to deter, threaten, inflict physical damage, harm, or [kill](/wiki/Death "Death"). Weapons are used to increase the efficacy and efficiency of activities such as [hunting](/wiki/Hunting "Hunting"), [crime](/wiki/Crime "Crime") (e.g., [murder](/wiki/Murder "Murder")), [law enforcement](/wiki/Law_enforcement "Law enforcement"), [self-defense](/wiki/Self-defense "Self-defense"), [warfare](/wiki/War "War"), or [suicide](/wiki/Suicide "Suicide"). In a broader context, weapons may be construed to include anything used to gain a tactical, strategic, material, or mental advantage over an adversary or enemy target.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

While ordinary objects such as [rocks](/wiki/Rock_(geology) "Rock (geology)") and [bottles](/wiki/Bottle "Bottle") can be [used as weapons](/wiki/Improvised_weapon "Improvised weapon"), many objects are expressly designed for the purpose; these range from simple implements such as [clubs](/wiki/Club_(weapon) "Club (weapon)") and [swords](/wiki/Sword "Sword") to complicated modern [firearms](/wiki/Firearm "Firearm"), [tanks](/wiki/Tank "Tank"), [missiles](/wiki/Missile "Missile") and [biological weapons](/wiki/Biological_warfare "Biological warfare"). Something that has been repurposed, converted, or enhanced to become a weapon of war is termed ***weaponized***, such as a [weaponized virus](/wiki/Biological_warfare "Biological warfare") or [weaponized laser](/wiki/Laser_weapon "Laser weapon").

The evolution of weaponry has been closely tied to advancements in technology and societal needs, with historical shifts from rudimentary tools to sophisticated systems reflecting broader changes in warfare and [security paradigms](/w/index.php?title=Security_paradigms&action=edit&redlink=1 "Security paradigms (page does not exist)").[[2]](#cite_note-2)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Weapon&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of weapons](/wiki/History_of_weapons "History of weapons")

The use of weapons has been a major driver of [cultural evolution](/wiki/Cultural_Evolution "Cultural Evolution") and [human history](/wiki/History "History") up to today since weapons are a type of tool that is used to dominate and subdue autonomous agents such as animals and, by doing so, allow for an expansion of the cultural niche, while simultaneously other weapon users (i.e., agents such as humans, groups, and cultures) can adapt to the weapons of enemies by learning, triggering a continuous process of competitive technological, skill, and cognitive improvement ([arms race](/wiki/Arms_race "Arms race")).[[3]](#cite_note-3)

### Prehistoric

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Weapon&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Prehistoric")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6b/N%C3%A9olithique_0001.jpg/250px-N%C3%A9olithique_0001.jpg)](/wiki/File:N%C3%A9olithique_0001.jpg)

An array of [Neolithic](/wiki/Neolithic "Neolithic") artifacts, including [bracelets](/wiki/Bracelet "Bracelet"), [axe](/wiki/Axe "Axe") heads, [chisels](/wiki/Chisel "Chisel"), and polishing tools.

The use of objects as weapons has been observed among [chimpanzees](/wiki/Common_Chimpanzee "Common Chimpanzee"),[[4]](#cite_note-Pruetz1-4) leading to speculation that early [hominids](/wiki/Hominids "Hominids") used weapons as early as five million years ago.[[5]](#cite_note-5) However, this cannot be confirmed using physical evidence because wooden clubs, spears, and unshaped stones would have left an ambiguous record. The earliest unambiguous weapons to be found are the [Schöningen spears](/wiki/Sch%C3%B6ningen_spears "Schöningen spears"), eight wooden throwing spears dating back more than 300,000 years.[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10) At the site of Nataruk in Turkana, Kenya, numerous human skeletons dating to 10,000 years ago may present evidence of traumatic injuries to the head, neck, ribs, knees, and hands, including [obsidian](/wiki/Obsidian "Obsidian") projectiles embedded in the bones that might have been caused by arrows and clubs during conflict between two hunter-gatherer groups.[[11]](#cite_note-11) But the interpretation of warfare at Nataruk has been challenged due to conflicting evidence.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

### Ancient history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Weapon&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Ancient history")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f7/Ballista-quadrirotis.jpeg/250px-Ballista-quadrirotis.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Ballista-quadrirotis.jpeg)

A four-wheeled [ballista](/wiki/Ballista "Ballista") drawn by armored [cataphract](/wiki/Cataphract "Cataphract") horses, c. 400

The earliest [ancient weapons](/wiki/List_of_ancient_weapons "List of ancient weapons") were evolutionary improvements of late [Neolithic](/wiki/Neolithic "Neolithic") implements, but significant improvements in materials and crafting techniques led to a series of revolutions in [military technology](/wiki/Military_technology "Military technology").

The development of metal tools began with [copper](/wiki/Copper "Copper") during the [Copper Age](/wiki/Copper_Age "Copper Age") (about 3,300 BC) and was followed by the [Bronze Age](/wiki/Bronze_Age "Bronze Age"), leading to the creation of the [Bronze Age sword](/wiki/Bronze_Age_sword "Bronze Age sword") and similar weapons.

During the Bronze Age, the first defensive structures and [fortifications](/wiki/Fortification "Fortification") appeared as well,[[13]](#cite_note-ashow-siegecraft-13) indicating an increased need for security. Weapons designed to breach fortifications followed soon after, such as the [battering ram](/wiki/Battering_ram "Battering ram"), which was in use by 2500 BC.[[13]](#cite_note-ashow-siegecraft-13)

The development of [ironworking](/wiki/History_of_ferrous_metallurgy "History of ferrous metallurgy") around 1300 BC in Greece had an important impact on the development of ancient weapons. It was not the introduction of early [Iron Age swords](/wiki/Iron_Age_sword "Iron Age sword"), however, as they were not superior to their bronze predecessors, but rather the [domestication of the horse](/wiki/Domestication_of_the_horse "Domestication of the horse") and widespread use of [spoked](/wiki/Spoke "Spoke") wheels by c. 2000 BC.[[14]](#cite_note-14) This led to the creation of the light, horse-drawn [chariot](/wiki/Chariot "Chariot"), whose improved mobility proved important during this era.[[15]](#cite_note-15) Spoke-wheeled chariot usage peaked around 1300 BC and then declined, ceasing to be militarily relevant by the 4th century BC.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

[Cavalry](/wiki/Cavalry "Cavalry") developed once horses were bred to support the weight of a human.[[17]](#cite_note-17) The horse extended the range and increased the speed of attacks.

Alexander's conquest saw the increased use of spears and shields in the Middle East and Western Asia; as Greek culture spread, many Greek and other European weapons came to be used in these regions. Many of these weapons were then adapted to fit their new uses in war.

In addition to land-based weaponry, [warships](/wiki/Warship "Warship"), such as the [trireme](/wiki/Trireme "Trireme"), were in use by the 7th century BC.[[18]](#cite_note-18)
During the first [First Punic War](/wiki/First_Punic_War "First Punic War"), the use of advanced warships contributed to a Roman victory over the Carthaginians.

### Post-classical history

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d3/Racinet1.jpg/250px-Racinet1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Racinet1.jpg)

Medieval Indian weapons

European warfare during [post-classical history](/wiki/Post-classical_history "Post-classical history") was dominated by elite groups of [knights](/wiki/Knights "Knights") supported by massed [infantry](/wiki/Infantry "Infantry"). They were involved in mobile combat and [sieges](/wiki/Sieges "Sieges"), which involved various [siege weapons](/wiki/Siege_weapon "Siege weapon") and tactics. Knights on horseback developed tactics for charging with [lances](/wiki/Lances "Lances"), providing an impact on the enemy formations, and then drawing more practical weapons (such as [swords](/wiki/Sword "Sword")) once they entered melee. By contrast, infantry, in the age before structured formations, relied on cheap, sturdy weapons such as [spears](/wiki/Spear "Spear") and billhooks in close combat and [bows](/wiki/Bow_(weapon) "Bow (weapon)") from a distance. As armies became more professional, their equipment was standardized, and infantry transitioned to [pikes](/wiki/Pike_(weapon) "Pike (weapon)"). Pikes are normally seven to eight feet in length and used in conjunction with smaller sidearms (short swords).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b4/Tower_of_London_interior.jpg/250px-Tower_of_London_interior.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tower_of_London_interior.jpg)

Ancient Chinese [cannon](/wiki/Cannon "Cannon") displayed in the [Tower of London](/wiki/Tower_of_London "Tower of London")

In Eastern and [Middle Eastern](/wiki/Middle_Eastern "Middle Eastern") warfare, similar tactics were developed independent of European influences.

The introduction of [gunpowder](/wiki/Gunpowder "Gunpowder") from Asia at the end of this period revolutionized warfare. Formations of [musketeers](/wiki/Musketeers "Musketeers"), protected by [pikemen](/wiki/Pikemen "Pikemen"), came to dominate open battles, and the [cannon](/wiki/Cannon "Cannon") replaced the [trebuchet](/wiki/Trebuchet "Trebuchet") as the dominant [siege weapon](/wiki/Siege_weapon "Siege weapon").
The Ottomans used the cannon to destroy much of the fortifications at Constantinople, which would change warfare as gunpowder became more available and technology improved.

#### Early modern

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Weapon&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Early modern")]

The European [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance") marked the beginning of the implementation of firearms in western warfare. [Guns](/wiki/Gun "Gun") and [rockets](/wiki/Rocket_(weapon) "Rocket (weapon)") were introduced to the battlefield.

[Firearms](/wiki/Firearm "Firearm") are qualitatively different from earlier weapons because they release energy from combustible [propellants](/wiki/Propellant "Propellant"), such as [gunpowder](/wiki/Gunpowder "Gunpowder"), rather than from a counterweight or spring. This energy is released very rapidly and can be replicated without much effort by the user. Therefore, even early firearms such as the [arquebus](/wiki/Arquebus "Arquebus") were much more powerful than human-powered weapons. Firearms became increasingly important and effective during the 16th–19th centuries, with progressive improvements in [ignition mechanisms](/wiki/Lock_(firearm) "Lock (firearm)") followed by revolutionary changes in [ammunition](/wiki/Ammunition "Ammunition") handling and propellant. During the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War"), new applications of firearms, including the [machine gun](/wiki/Machine_gun "Machine gun") and [ironclad warship](/wiki/Ironclad_warship "Ironclad warship"), emerged that would still be recognizable and useful military weapons today, particularly in [limited conflicts](/wiki/Limited_war "Limited war"). In the 19th century, [warship](/wiki/Warship "Warship") propulsion changed from [sail](/wiki/Sail "Sail") power to [fossil fuel](/wiki/Fossil_fuel "Fossil fuel")-powered [steam engines](/wiki/Steam_engine "Steam engine").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/75/Prussian_bayonet_clean.jpg/500px-Prussian_bayonet_clean.jpg)](/wiki/File:Prussian_bayonet_clean.jpg)

The [bayonet](/wiki/Bayonet "Bayonet") is used as both a [knife](/wiki/Knife "Knife") and, when attached to the front of a rifle, a [polearm](/wiki/Polearm "Polearm").

Since the mid-18th century North American French-Indian war through the beginning of the 20th century, human-powered weapons were reduced from the primary weaponry of the battlefield to yielding gunpowder-based weaponry. Sometimes referred to as the "Age of Rifles",[[19]](#cite_note-19) this period was characterized by the development of firearms for infantry and cannons for support, as well as the beginnings of mechanized weapons such as the [machine gun](/wiki/Machine_gun "Machine gun"). Artillery pieces such as [howitzers](/wiki/Howitzer "Howitzer") were able to destroy masonry fortresses and other fortifications, and this single invention caused a [revolution in military affairs](/wiki/Revolution_in_military_affairs "Revolution in military affairs"), establishing tactics and doctrine that are still in use today.

#### World War I

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Weapon&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: World War I")]

See also: [Military technology during World War I](/wiki/Military_technology_during_World_War_I "Military technology during World War I")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/Vickers_machine_gun_in_the_Battle_of_Passchendaele_-_September_1917.jpg/250px-Vickers_machine_gun_in_the_Battle_of_Passchendaele_-_September_1917.jpg)](/wiki/File:Vickers_machine_gun_in_the_Battle_of_Passchendaele_-_September_1917.jpg)

The [Vickers](/wiki/Vickers_machine_gun "Vickers machine gun") was the successor to the [Maxim gun](/wiki/Maxim_gun "Maxim gun") and remained in British military service for 79 consecutive years.

An important feature of [industrial age](/wiki/Industrial_age "Industrial age") warfare was [technological escalation](/wiki/Technological_escalation "Technological escalation") – innovations were rapidly matched through replication or countered by another innovation.

[World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") marked the entry of fully industrialized warfare as well as [weapons of mass destruction](/wiki/Weapons_of_mass_destruction "Weapons of mass destruction") (*e.g.*, [chemical](/wiki/Chemical_weapon "Chemical weapon") and [biological weapons](/wiki/Biological_agent "Biological agent")), and new weapons were developed quickly to meet wartime needs. The [technological escalation during World War I](/wiki/Technology_during_World_War_I "Technology during World War I") was profound, including the wide introduction of [aircraft](/wiki/Aircraft "Aircraft") into [warfare](/wiki/Technology_during_World_War_I "Technology during World War I") and naval warfare with the introduction of [aircraft carriers](/wiki/Aircraft_carrier "Aircraft carrier"). Above all, it promised the military commanders independence from horses and a resurgence in [maneuver warfare](/wiki/Maneuver_warfare "Maneuver warfare") through the extensive use of motor vehicles. The changes that these military technologies underwent were evolutionary but defined their development for the rest of the century.[*[This paragraph needs citation(s)](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### Interwar

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Weapon&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Interwar")]

This period of innovation in weapon design continued in the interwar period (between WWI and WWII) with the continuous evolution of weapon systems by all major industrial powers. The major armament firms were [Schneider-Creusot](/wiki/Schneider-Creusot "Schneider-Creusot") (based in France), [Škoda Works](/wiki/%C5%A0koda_Works "Škoda Works") (Czechoslovakia), and [Vickers](/wiki/Vickers "Vickers") (Great Britain). The 1920s were committed to [disarmament](/wiki/Disarmament "Disarmament") and the outlawing of war and poison gas, but rearmament picked up rapidly in the 1930s. The munitions makers responded nimbly to the rapidly shifting strategic and economic landscape. The main purchasers of munitions from the big three companies were Romania, Yugoslavia, Greece, and Turkey – and, to a lesser extent, Poland, Finland, the Baltic States, and the Soviet Union.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

##### Criminalizing poison gas

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Weapon&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Criminalizing poison gas")]

Realistic critics understood that war could not really be outlawed, but its worst excesses might be banned. [Poison gas](/wiki/Poison_gas "Poison gas") became the focus of a worldwide crusade in the 1920s. Poison gas did not win battles, and the generals did not want it. The soldiers hated it far more intensely than bullets or explosive shells. By 1918, chemical shells made up 35 percent of French ammunition supplies, 25 percent of British, and 20 percent of American stock. The "Protocol for the Prohibition of the Use in War of Asphyxiating, Poisonous, or Other Gases and of Bacteriological Methods of Warfare", also known as the [Geneva Protocol](/wiki/Geneva_Protocol "Geneva Protocol"), was issued in 1925 and was accepted as policy by all major countries. In 1937, poison gas was manufactured in large quantities but not used except against nations that lacked modern weapons or gas masks.[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22)

#### World War II and postwar

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Weapon&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: World War II and postwar")]

See also: [Military technology during World War II](/wiki/Military_technology_during_World_War_II "Military technology during World War II")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1a/Victory_Show_Cosby_UK_06-09-2015_WW2_re-enactment_Trade_stalls_Militaria_personal_gear_replicas_reprod.originals_zaphad1_Flickr_CCBY2.0_Misc._machine_guns_weapons_etc_IMG_3874.jpg/250px-thumbnail.jpg)](/wiki/File:Victory_Show_Cosby_UK_06-09-2015_WW2_re-enactment_Trade_stalls_Militaria_personal_gear_replicas_reprod.originals_zaphad1_Flickr_CCBY2.0_Misc._machine_guns_weapons_etc_IMG_3874.jpg)

A variety of firearms from the World War II and postwar eras on a [firearm rack](/wiki/Firearm_rack "Firearm rack")

Many modern military weapons, particularly ground-based ones, are relatively minor improvements to weapon systems developed during World War II. World War II marked perhaps the most frantic period of weapon development in the history of humanity. Massive numbers of new designs and concepts were fielded, and all existing technologies were improved between 1939 and 1945. The most powerful weapon invented during this period was the [nuclear bomb](/wiki/Nuclear_weapon "Nuclear weapon"); however, many other weapons influenced the world, such as [jet aircraft](/wiki/Jet_aircraft "Jet aircraft") and [radar](/wiki/Radar "Radar"), but were overshadowed by the visibility of nuclear weapons and long-range rockets.[*[This paragraph needs citation(s)](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### Nuclear weapons

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Weapon&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Nuclear weapons")]

Main article: [Nuclear holocaust](/wiki/Nuclear_holocaust "Nuclear holocaust")

Since the realization of [mutual assured destruction](/wiki/Mutual_assured_destruction "Mutual assured destruction") (MAD), the nuclear option of all-out war is no longer considered a survivable scenario. During the [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War") in the years following World War II, both the United States and the Soviet Union engaged in a [nuclear arms race](/wiki/Nuclear_arms_race "Nuclear arms race"). Each country and their allies continually attempted to out-develop each other in the field of nuclear armaments. Once the joint technological capabilities reached the point of being able to ensure the destruction of the Earth by 100 fold, a new tactic had to be developed. With this realization, armaments development funding shifted back to primarily sponsoring the development of conventional arms technologies for support of [limited wars](/wiki/Limited_war "Limited war") rather than [total war](/wiki/Total_war "Total war").[[23]](#cite_note-NuclearDefunding-23)

## International arm cooperation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Weapon&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: International arm cooperation")]

It is essential to highlight the growing relevance of international military cooperation and the global diffusion of deterrence strategies. As outlined in Baizidi's theory on deterrence, the internationalization of deterrence involves the transfer of arms and other military capabilities to aligned states and non-state actors, as a means of amplifying strategic reach and threat credibility.[[24]](#cite_note-:0-24)

In today’s interconnected security environment, the deterrent value of weapons often hinges not only on their technical specifications but also on how they are deployed collaboratively across borders. Missile sharing arrangements, forward-defense networks, and alliance-building exemplify how arm's cooperation has shifted from a purely national doctrine toward a regional and even global concept. Such internationalized deterrence mechanisms, both conventional and unconventional, reshape strategic calculations and expand the zones of influence — redefining the tactical significance of weapons far beyond their immediate geography.[[24]](#cite_note-:0-24)

## Types

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Weapon&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Types")]

### By user

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Weapon&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: By user")]

:   *– what person or unit uses the weapon*

* [Personal weapons](/w/index.php?title=Personal_weapon&action=edit&redlink=1 "Personal weapon (page does not exist)") (or [small arms](/wiki/Small_arms "Small arms")) – designed to be used by a single person.
* [Light weapons](/wiki/SALW "SALW") – 'man-portable' weapons that may require a small team to operate.[[25]](#cite_note-25)
* Heavy weapons – [artillery](/wiki/Artillery "Artillery") and similar weapons larger than light weapons (see [SALW](/wiki/SALW "SALW")).
* [Crew served weapons](/wiki/Crew_served_weapon "Crew served weapon") – larger than personal weapons, requiring two or more people to operate correctly.
* Fortification weapons – mounted in a permanent installation or used primarily within a fortification.
* [Mountain weapons](/wiki/Mountain_gun "Mountain gun") – for use by mountain forces or those operating in difficult terrain.
* [Vehicle-mounted weapons](/w/index.php?title=Category:Vehicle-mounted_weaponss&action=edit&redlink=1 "Category:Vehicle-mounted weaponss (page does not exist)") – to be mounted on any type of [combat vehicle](/wiki/Combat_vehicle "Combat vehicle").
* [Railway weapons](/wiki/Railway_weapon "Railway weapon") – designed to be mounted on railway cars, including armored trains.
* [Aircraft weapons](/wiki/List_of_aircraft_weapons "List of aircraft weapons") – carried on and used by some type of [aircraft](/wiki/Aircraft "Aircraft"), [helicopter](/wiki/Helicopter "Helicopter"), or other aerial vehicle.
* [Naval weapons](/wiki/Category:Naval_weapons "Category:Naval weapons") – mounted on [ships](/wiki/Ship "Ship") and [submarines](/wiki/Submarine "Submarine").
* [Space weapons](/wiki/Space_weapon "Space weapon") – are designed to be used in or launched from space.
* [Autonomous weapons](/wiki/Autonomous_weapon "Autonomous weapon") – are capable of accomplishing a mission with limited or no human intervention.

### By function

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Weapon&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: By function")]

:   *– the construction of the weapon and the principle of operation*

* [Antimatter weapons](/wiki/Antimatter_weapon "Antimatter weapon") (theoretical) – would combine matter and [antimatter](/wiki/Antimatter "Antimatter") to cause a powerful explosion.
* [Archery](/wiki/Archery "Archery") weapons – operate by using a tensioned string and a bent solid to launch a projectile.
* [Artillery](/wiki/Artillery "Artillery") – firearms capable of launching heavy projectiles over long distances.
* [Biological weapons](/wiki/Biological_weapon "Biological weapon") – spread biological agents, causing disease or infection.
* [Blunt instruments](/wiki/Blunt_instrument "Blunt instrument") – designed to break or fracture bones, produce [concussions](/wiki/Concussion "Concussion"), create organ ruptures, or [crush injuries](/wiki/Crush_injury "Crush injury").
* [Chemical weapons](/wiki/Chemical_weapon "Chemical weapon") – poison people and cause reactions.
* [Edged and bladed weapons](/wiki/Edged_and_bladed_weapons "Edged and bladed weapons") – designed to pierce or cut through skin, muscle, or bone and cause internal or external bleeding.
* [Energy weapons](/wiki/Directed-energy_weapon "Directed-energy weapon") – rely on concentrating forms of energy to attack, such as lasers or sonic attacks.
* [Explosive weapons](/wiki/Explosive_weapon "Explosive weapon") – use a physical explosion to create a blast, concussion, or spread [shrapnel](/wiki/Shrapnel_(fragment) "Shrapnel (fragment)").
* [Firearms](/wiki/Firearms "Firearms") – use a chemical charge to launch projectiles.
* [Improvised weapons](/wiki/Improvised_weapon "Improvised weapon") – common objects reused as weapons, such as crowbars and kitchen knives.
* [Incendiary weapons](/wiki/Incendiary_weapon "Incendiary weapon") – cause damage by fire.
* [Loitering munitions](/wiki/Loitering_munition "Loitering munition") – designed to [loiter](/wiki/Loiter_(aeronautics) "Loiter (aeronautics)") over a battlefield, striking once a target is located.
* [Magnetic weapons](/wiki/Magnetic_weapon "Magnetic weapon") – use magnetic fields to propel projectiles or focus particle beams.
* [Missiles](/wiki/Missile "Missile") – rockets that are guided to their target after launch. (Also a general term for [projectile](/wiki/Projectile "Projectile") weapons.)
* [Non-lethal weapons](/wiki/Non-lethal_weapon "Non-lethal weapon") – designed to subdue without killing.
* [Nuclear weapons](/wiki/Nuclear_weapon "Nuclear weapon") – use radioactive materials to create [nuclear fission](/wiki/Nuclear_fission "Nuclear fission") or [nuclear fusion](/wiki/Nuclear_fusion "Nuclear fusion") detonations
* [Rockets](/wiki/Rocket_(weapon) "Rocket (weapon)") – self-propelled projectiles.
* [Suicide weapons](/wiki/Suicide_weapon "Suicide weapon") – exploit the willingness of their operators not surviving the attack.

### By target

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Weapon&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: By target")]

:   *– the type of target the weapon is designed to attack*

* [Anti-aircraft](/wiki/Anti-aircraft "Anti-aircraft") weapons – target missiles and aerial vehicles in flight.
* [Anti-fortification weapons](/wiki/Anti-fortification_weapon "Anti-fortification weapon") – designed to target enemy installations.
* [Anti-personnel weapons](/wiki/Anti-personnel_weapon "Anti-personnel weapon") – designed to attack people, either individually or in numbers.
* [Anti-radiation](/wiki/Anti-radiation_missile "Anti-radiation missile") weapons – target sources of electronic radiation, particularly radar emitters.
* [Anti-satellite weapons](/wiki/Anti-satellite_weapon "Anti-satellite weapon") – target orbiting satellites.
* [Anti-ship weapons](/wiki/Category:Anti-ship_weapons "Category:Anti-ship weapons") – target ships and vessels on water.
* [Anti-submarine weapons](/wiki/Anti-submarine_weapon "Anti-submarine weapon") – target submarines and other underwater targets.
* [Anti-tank weapons](/wiki/Anti-tank_weapon "Anti-tank weapon") – designed to defeat armored targets.
* [Area denial weapons](/wiki/Area_denial_weapons "Area denial weapons") – target territory, making it unsafe or unsuitable for enemy use or travel.
* [Hunting weapons](/wiki/Hunting_weapon "Hunting weapon") – weapons used to hunt [game animals](/wiki/Game_animal "Game animal").
* [Infantry support weapons](/wiki/Category:Infantry_support_weapons "Category:Infantry support weapons") – designed to attack various threats to infantry units.
* [Siege engines](/wiki/Siege_engine "Siege engine") – designed to break or circumvent heavy fortifications in [siege warfare](/wiki/Siege_warfare "Siege warfare").

## Manufacture of weapons

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Weapon&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Manufacture of weapons")]

Main article: [Arms industry](/wiki/Arms_industry "Arms industry")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e3/Pantex_ordance_plant_1944-01-26.jpg/250px-Pantex_ordance_plant_1944-01-26.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pantex_ordance_plant_1944-01-26.jpg)

Ordnance being produced at [Pantex](/wiki/Pantex "Pantex") in [Amarillo](/wiki/Amarillo,_Texas "Amarillo, Texas"), [Texas](/wiki/Texas "Texas"), United States, during World War II

The arms industry is a global industry that involves the sale and manufacture of weaponry. It consists of a [commercial](/wiki/Commerce "Commerce") [industry](/wiki/Industry_(economics) "Industry (economics)") involved in the [research and development](/wiki/Research_and_development "Research and development"), [engineering](/wiki/Engineering "Engineering"), production, and servicing of [military](/wiki/Military "Military") material, equipment, and facilities. Many [industrialized countries](/wiki/Industrialized_countries "Industrialized countries") have a domestic arms industry to supply their own military forces, and some also have a substantial trade in weapons for use by their citizens for self-defense, hunting, or sporting purposes.

Contracts to supply a given country's military are awarded by governments, making arms contracts of substantial political importance. The link between politics and the arms trade can result in the development of a "[military–industrial complex](/wiki/Military%E2%80%93industrial_complex "Military–industrial complex")", where the armed forces, commerce, and politics become closely linked.

According to research institute SIPRI, the volume of international transfers of major weapons in 2010–2014 was 16 percent higher than in 2005–2009,[[26]](#cite_note-26) and the arms sales of the world's 100 largest private arms-producing and military services companies totaled $420 billion in 2018.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

## Legislation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Weapon&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Legislation")]

The production, possession, trade, and use of many weapons are controlled. This may be at a [local](/wiki/Local_government "Local government") or [central government](/wiki/Central_government "Central government") level or by international treaty. Examples of such controls include:

* The [right of self-defense](/wiki/Right_of_self-defense "Right of self-defense")
* [Knife legislation](/wiki/Knife_legislation "Knife legislation")
* [Air gun laws](/wiki/Air_gun_laws "Air gun laws")
* [Gun law](/wiki/Gun_law "Gun law")
* [Arms trafficking](/wiki/Arms_trafficking "Arms trafficking") laws
* [Arms control](/wiki/Arms_control "Arms control") treaties
* [Space Preservation Treaty](/wiki/Space_Preservation_Treaty "Space Preservation Treaty")

### Gun laws

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Weapon&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Gun laws")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c4/Yellow_Vests_-_Act_19.jpg/250px-Yellow_Vests_-_Act_19.jpg)](/wiki/File:Yellow_Vests_-_Act_19.jpg)

[Tear gas](/wiki/Tear_gas "Tear gas") used by [riot police](/wiki/Riot_police "Riot police")

Main article: [Gun laws](/wiki/Gun_laws "Gun laws")

All countries have laws and policies regulating aspects such as the manufacture, sale, transfer, possession, modification, and use of small arms by civilians.

Countries that regulate access to firearms will typically restrict access to certain categories of firearms and then restrict the categories of persons who may be granted a license for access to such firearms. There may be separate licenses for hunting, sport shooting (a.k.a. target shooting), self-defense, collecting, and concealed carry, with different sets of requirements, permissions, and responsibilities.

### Arms control laws

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Weapon&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Arms control laws")]

Main article: [Arms control](/wiki/Arms_control "Arms control")

International treaties and agreements place restrictions on the development, production, stockpiling, proliferation, and usage of weapons, from [small arms](/wiki/Small_arms "Small arms") and [heavy weapons](/wiki/Artillery "Artillery") to [weapons of mass destruction](/wiki/Weapons_of_mass_destruction "Weapons of mass destruction"). Arms control is typically exercised through the use of diplomacy, which seeks to impose such limitations upon consenting participants, although it may also comprise efforts by a nation or group of nations to enforce limitations upon a non-consenting country.

### Arms trafficking laws

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Weapon&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Arms trafficking laws")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [[icon]](/wiki/File:Wiki_letter_w_cropped.svg) | This section **needs expansion**. You can help by [adding missing information](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Weapon&action=edit&section=). *(May 2018)* |

Main article: [Arms trafficking](/wiki/Arms_trafficking "Arms trafficking")

Arms trafficking is the trafficking of contraband weapons and [ammunition](/wiki/Ammunition "Ammunition"). What constitutes legal trade in firearms varies widely, depending on local and national laws. In 2001, the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") had made a [protocol](/wiki/Protocol_Against_the_Illicit_Manufacturing_of_and_Trafficking_in_Firearms "Protocol Against the Illicit Manufacturing of and Trafficking in Firearms") against the manufacturing and trafficking of illicit arms. This protocol made governments dispose illegal arms, and to license new firearms being produced, to ensure them being legitimate.[[28]](#cite_note-28) It was signed by 122 parties.

## Lifecycle problems

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Weapon&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Lifecycle problems")]

There are a number of issues around the potential ongoing risks from deployed weapons, the safe storage of weapons, and their eventual disposal when they are no longer effective or safe.

* [Ocean dumping](/wiki/Ocean_dumping "Ocean dumping") of unused weapons such as bombs, ordnance, landmines, and chemical weapons has been common practice by many nations and has created hazards.[[29]](#cite_note-29)[[30]](#cite_note-30)[[31]](#cite_note-me-31)[[32]](#cite_note-wbgh-32)
* [Unexploded ordnance](/wiki/Unexploded_ordnance "Unexploded ordnance") (UXO) are bombs, land mines, naval mines, and similar devices that did not explode when they were employed and still pose a risk for many years or decades.
* [Demining](/wiki/Demining "Demining") or mine clearance from areas of past conflict is a difficult process, but every year, landmines kill 15,000 to 20,000 people and severely maim countless more.[[33]](#cite_note-33)
* [Nuclear terrorism](/wiki/Nuclear_terrorism "Nuclear terrorism") was a serious concern after the fall of the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union"), with the prospect of "loose nukes" being available.[[34]](#cite_note-34) While this risk may have receded,[[35]](#cite_note-35) similar situations may arise in the future.

## In science fiction

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Weapon&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: In science fiction")]

Main article: [Weapons in science fiction](/wiki/Weapons_in_science_fiction "Weapons in science fiction")

Strange and exotic weapons are a recurring feature or theme in [science fiction](/wiki/Science_fiction "Science fiction"). In some cases, weapons first introduced in science fiction have now become a reality. Other science fiction weapons, such as [force fields](/wiki/Force_field_(technology) "Force field (technology)") and [stasis fields](/wiki/Stasis_field "Stasis field"), remain purely fictional and are often beyond the realms of known physical possibility.

At its most prosaic, science fiction features an endless variety of [sidearms](/wiki/Sidearm_(weapon) "Sidearm (weapon)"), mostly variations on real weapons such as [guns](/wiki/Gun "Gun") and [swords](/wiki/Sword "Sword"). Among the best-known of these are the [phaser](/wiki/Weapons_in_Star_Trek#Phaser "Weapons in Star Trek") used in the *[Star Trek](/wiki/Star_Trek "Star Trek")* television series, films, and novels, and the [lightsaber](/wiki/Lightsaber "Lightsaber") and [blaster](/wiki/Blaster_(Star_Wars) "Blaster (Star Wars)") featured in the *[Star Wars](/wiki/Star_Wars "Star Wars")* movies, comics, novels, and TV series.

In addition to adding action and entertainment value, weaponry in science fiction sometimes becomes a theme when it touches on deeper concerns, often motivated by contemporary issues. One example is science fiction that deals with [weapons of mass destruction](/wiki/Weapons_of_mass_destruction "Weapons of mass destruction") like [doomsday devices](/wiki/List_of_fictional_doomsday_devices "List of fictional doomsday devices").
